author: Ravi Bheesetty
description: A three-layer measurement framework for AI products and AI features: Model Performance, Product Behaviour, and Business Outcome. Industry-agnostic by design — no built-in weighting assumes a particular sector. Applies at the level of a single AI feature, or a combination of AI features within one product. Encodes a fixed rubric, a shared maturity ladder, a verdict taxonomy, and a set of vanity-metric probes so an agent can triage what's tracked, flag what's missing, and explain why it matters. The scoring math in this file is deterministic; only the per-layer 1-5 judgment (evidence review) is left to the calling agent.
implementation_notes: This file is the single source of truth. framework.yaml is a generated, read-only mirror produced by scripts/sync_framework_yaml.py — never hand-edited, regenerated after every change to this file. usecases/*.py in both the Python and Node packages must read thresholds, weights, and verdict rules from this file at runtime or build time — never hardcode a duplicate value. docs/mcp.md is generated from code, which is generated from this file, so none of the three can drift the way EvalSurfer's README and docs/mcp.md did.

unit_of_assessment.feature: A single AI capability — one model, one workflow, one surfaced feature. This is the base unit the layers and indicators below describe directly.
unit_of_assessment.product: A combination of AI features within one product. Assessed by running the feature-level triage independently per feature, then rolling results up using the rules in the aggregation section below. Not a separate rubric — the same layers, indicators, and verdicts apply to each feature before rollup.
unit_of_assessment.industry_scope: None built in, on purpose. v1 ships with equal, fixed layer weights (see scoring.overall_score) and no sector-specific calibration. See scoring.overall_score.weights_extensibility for how sector weighting could be reintroduced later, once there's evidence to calibrate it against rather than defaults asserted without one.

layers.name: Model Performance
layers.question: Is the AI doing what it was built to do?

indicators.name: Task Success Rate
indicators.description: Percentage of AI outputs that correctly complete the intended task, measured against a defined pass/fail rubric for that task.

indicators.name: Confidence Calibration
indicators.description: How closely the model's expressed or implied confidence tracks its actual correctness. Poor calibration means the model is as confident when wrong as when right.

indicators.name: Hallucination Rate
indicators.description: Percentage of outputs containing claims not supported by the input, retrieved context, or ground truth.

indicators.name: Grounding Coverage
indicators.description: For retrieval- or tool-using systems, the percentage of factual claims traceable to a cited source.
indicators.applicable_if: system uses retrieval or tool calls

layers.name: Product Behaviour
layers.question: Are users interacting with AI output the way you designed for?

indicators.name: Adoption Rate
indicators.description: Percentage of eligible users or sessions that engage with the AI feature at all, out of those exposed to it.

indicators.name: Override Rate
indicators.description: Percentage of AI outputs that users manually edit, reject, or reverse before acting on them.

risk_threshold.note: Empirically, override rates at or above this level predict abandonment before they show up in churn numbers. Referenced directly by the trust_failure_signal verdict below.

indicators.name: Repeat Use Rate
indicators.description: Percentage of users who use the AI feature again within a defined window after first use.

indicators.name: Escalation Rate
indicators.description: Percentage of AI-handled interactions that get escalated to a human or a fallback path.

layers.name: Business Outcome
layers.question: Is the AI feature moving the business metric it was funded to move?

indicators.name: Attributed Business Impact
indicators.description: Movement in the specific business metric this feature was funded against — revenue, conversion, retention, or cost, named per deployment rather than fixed here.

indicators.name: Cost-to-Serve Delta
indicators.description: Change in fully-loaded unit cost per interaction — compute, human review, and support — after the AI feature shipped.

indicators.name: Time to Value
indicators.description: Change in time from user intent to realized outcome, with the AI feature versus without it.

indicators.name: Retention or Renewal Impact
indicators.description: Effect on renewal, churn, or retention attributable to the AI feature.

maturity_ladder.label: No Tracking
maturity_ladder.description: No indicator in this layer has a defined metric, an owner, or any logging in place.

maturity_ladder.label: Ad Hoc
maturity_ladder.description: At least one indicator is informally observed — a dashboard glance, a gut check — but not systematically logged or reviewed on any cadence.

maturity_ladder.label: Instrumented
maturity_ladder.description: Indicators are logged with explicit targets, but review is irregular and not yet tied to a specific decision.

maturity_ladder.label: Operationalized
maturity_ladder.description: Indicators are reviewed on a fixed cadence and have influenced at least one product or business decision in the last quarter.

maturity_ladder.label: Closed-Loop
maturity_ladder.description: Indicators feed a defined feedback mechanism — an experiment, a retraining trigger, a pricing or roadmap decision — with evidence the loop has fired at least once.

scoring.layer_score_note: The deterministic layer here stops at giving the agent the rubric. The agent reviews whatever evidence the user provides and assigns 1-5 per layer against the maturity_ladder anchors. Everything after that score exists — coverage math, verdicts, aggregation — is deterministic.

overall_score.weights_extensibility: Fixed and equal in v1, deliberately. Sector-specific weighting was cut from this version because the numbers weren't derived from real evidence — see CONTRIBUTING.md before proposing calibrated weights. A future calibration_status field and evidence-backed profiles can be reintroduced once golden-set cases exist to derive real numbers from, not before.
overall_score.purpose: A continuous read of overall measurement health, equally sensitive to all three layers regardless of industry or feature type.

maturity_level.purpose: The Triangle is only as strong as its weakest vertex. This deliberately does not average a blind spot away — a business cannot claim Level 4 maturity while one layer is still untracked.

aggregation.applies_when: unit_of_assessment scope is "product" — rolling up two or more feature-level triage results into one product-level pulse.

product_maturity_level.rationale: Same weakest-link principle as layer-level maturity_level. A product with four well-instrumented AI features and one completely blind feature is not a Level 4 product — this doesn't average the blind spot away, consistent with the Triangle's own logic.

product_overall_score.rationale: Unlike maturity_level, this single continuous number is allowed to average — it's meant to read as a portfolio health gauge, not a pass/fail gate.

verdict_rollup.rule: Verdicts do not blend across features. Every verdict that fires at the feature level is retained and attributed to that specific feature in the product-level report.
verdict_rollup.rationale: A single 'Balanced' product-level verdict could otherwise hide one feature with Business-Outcome Orphaned sitting next to three well-instrumented ones. The report lists which feature triggered which verdict; it does not summarize them away.

verdicts.name: Balanced
verdicts.description: All three layers are at least instrumented. This is a pass, not a ceiling — Operationalized and Closed-Loop are still worth pursuing.
investigative_questions: Which layer has the lowest score, and what's the smallest step that would move it up one rung on the maturity ladder?
Is any layer's instrumentation reviewed only because this triage prompted someone to look, or is there a standing cadence?

verdicts.name: Business-Outcome Orphaned
verdicts.trigger_description: Business Outcome layer scores exactly 1 (No Tracking), regardless of the other two layers.
verdicts.description: The AI feature has no defined link to a business metric at all. This is the single most common and most expensive gap seen in practice — a feature can be well-built and well-used and still get cut in a budget review because nobody can point to what it moved.
investigative_questions: What business metric was this feature's original business case or roadmap justification built on?
Who owns the number this feature was supposed to move, and have they been asked whether it moved?
If no such metric was ever defined, was this feature funded against a real business hypothesis, or built because it seemed worth doing?

verdicts.name: Trust Failure Signal
verdicts.trigger_description: Model Performance scores 4 or higher while the override_rate indicator has crossed its own risk_threshold (defined on the indicator, currently >= 40%).
verdicts.description: The model measures well internally, but users are overriding it faster than the threshold that historically predicts abandonment. This is a leading indicator, not a lagging one — it shows up here before it shows up as churn.

cross_reference.author: Ravi Bheesetty
cross_reference.relationship: This verdict is an early-warning instrument for the Trust Failure node in the AI Product Failure Triangle. Same failure mode, caught in the metrics instead of in the churn report.

investigative_questions: What changed in the model, the UI, or the surrounding workflow right before the override rate crossed the risk threshold?
Are users overriding the output because it's wrong, or because it's right but not yet trusted?
Has anyone actually read a sample of the overridden outputs, or is this purely a rate observed from telemetry?

verdicts.name: Vanity-Metric Risk
verdicts.trigger_description: Business Outcome scores exactly 2 (Ad Hoc) while Model Performance or Product Behaviour scores 4 or higher.
verdicts.description: Business Outcome is nominally tracked but shallow, sitting next to layers that are heavily instrumented. The gap reads as smaller than it is — there's a metric, it's just decorative. Distinct from Business-Outcome Orphaned, which is a harder floor of zero tracking.
investigative_questions: If the business outcome number were removed from the dashboard tomorrow, would anyone notice it was gone?
Is it reviewed on the same cadence as the other two layers, or does it only appear in a quarterly slide?
Was a target or threshold ever set for it, or is it tracked without anything that would trigger action?

verdicts.name: Blind Spot
verdicts.trigger_description: Evaluated once per layer, excluding business_outcome — that layer at value 1 is always represented by Business-Outcome Orphaned instead, never by this generic flag, so the same fact isn't reported twice under two names. Fires when a non-excluded layer scores exactly 1 while at least one other layer scores 3 or higher — i.e., the team is clearly capable of instrumenting, and chose not to for this layer.
verdicts.description: A generic, per-layer flag for Model Performance or Product Behaviour going untracked. Business Outcome going untracked is always Business-Outcome Orphaned instead — that's the named specialization for the costliest case, and it fully replaces this generic flag for that one layer rather than co-firing alongside it.
investigative_questions: Given {layer_name} is untracked while another layer is solid, was that a deliberate scoping decision or an oversight?
What's the smallest instrumentation step that would move {layer_name} from No Tracking to Ad Hoc?

vanity_metric_probes.pattern: DAU/MAU or raw usage volume of the AI feature, reported as evidence for Product Behaviour.
vanity_metric_probes.why_it_fails: Exposure and volume measure reach, not whether the output changed how the user acted. A feature can carry high usage and a near-100% override rate at the same time.

vanity_metric_probes.pattern: Model accuracy or an internal eval-set score, reported as evidence for Business Outcome.
vanity_metric_probes.why_it_fails: This is Model Performance evidence, not Business Outcome evidence, no matter how good the score is. A model can score 95% on an eval set and move zero business metrics if nobody acts on its output.

vanity_metric_probes.pattern: Number of AI-generated outputs shipped, drafts produced, or automations triggered, presented as evidence for any layer.
vanity_metric_probes.why_it_fails: Output volume is an activity metric. It says the system ran, not that it worked, was trusted, or mattered.

vanity_metric_probes.pattern: Positive sentiment from a user survey, reported alone as a trust signal.
vanity_metric_probes.why_it_fails: Self-reported sentiment is weak evidence on its own. It should be corroborated by behavioral evidence — override rate, repeat use — before it counts as a tracked Product Behaviour indicator.

cross_references.author: Ravi Bheesetty
cross_references.relationship: See the trust_failure_signal verdict above — it operationalizes the Trust Failure node as a measurable, early-firing condition rather than a retrospective label.
